8.2: Hallucination Waiver

(a) Acknowledgment.
You expressly acknowledge and accept that Output generated by the Service may contain Hallucinations (as defined in Section 1.8) — including factually incorrect statements, fabricated citations, invented statistics, false attributions, and references to fictional entities, events, or sources.

(b) Probabilistic Nature.
These inaccuracies arise from the probabilistic nature of generative AI (as described in Section 2.1) and are not defects in the Service. They are an inherent characteristic of the technology. Provider has disclosed this characteristic to you before your acceptance of this Agreement.

(c) Waiver.
To the maximum extent permitted by Applicable Law, you waive all claims against Provider arising from or related to your reliance on Output that contains Hallucinations — provided that Provider has complied with its disclosure obligations in this Agreement. This waiver applies regardless of the nature or severity of the Hallucination and regardless of the damages claimed.

(d) Condition Precedent.
This waiver is supported by the following conditions, all of which have been met:

    i.   Provider disclosed the risk of Hallucination in the Preamble, in the definition (Section 1.8), and in the Service description (Section 2.1);

    ii.  Provider required you to implement HITL (Section 5.1) and to verify Output (Section 5.3) before reliance;

    iii. Provider listed specific verification obligations (Section 5.3(b)) corresponding to known Hallucination categories; and

    iv.  you accepted this Agreement with knowledge of all of the above.

annotations.label: Legal problem
annotations.text: The risk is not simply “AI may be wrong.” The legal risk is user reliance on a confident wrong output, especially where the AI appears to speak with company authority.

annotations.label: Defensive control
annotations.text: The clause works as a four-layer defense: acknowledgment, technical explanation, waiver, and condition precedent tied to disclosure + HITL + verification.

annotations.label: Instrument route

annotations.label: Reviewer note

triggers.main_clause:
SECTION 4: FINANCIAL CIRCUIT BREAKER

This section installs a spending brake on your Agent. You set the maximum amount it can spend per session and per calendar period. When it hits the limit, it stops. Think of it as a credit card limit for your AI — except this one actually enforces itself.

4.1: Per-Session Cap

Each Autonomous Agent operating at Tier 3 (Transact) or above must have a Circuit Breaker Threshold for each Session. When the aggregate monetary value of Actions in a single Session reaches the threshold, the Agent is automatically paused. No further Actions may be executed until Customer manually resumes the Agent.

4.2: Per-Period Cap

Customer must configure at least one calendar-period Circuit Breaker Threshold (daily, weekly, or monthly). When the aggregate monetary value of Actions across all Sessions in the selected period reaches the threshold, all Agents operating at Tier 3 or above are paused until the period resets or Customer manually overrides.

4.3: Scope of Monetary Actions

Circuit Breaker Thresholds apply to all Actions with a monetary component, including but not limited to: direct payments, subscription enrollments, resource provisioning (cloud compute, storage, bandwidth), advertising spend, inventory purchases, and API usage fees charged by third-party systems.

4.4: Customer Configuration Responsibility

Customer is solely responsible for setting appropriate Circuit Breaker Thresholds. Default thresholds are provided in Schedule C. If Customer modifies the defaults, Customer accepts responsibility for the modified thresholds.

4.5: Provider Liability

Provider is liable under the Negligence Exception (Section 2.3) only if the Circuit Breaker mechanism itself malfunctions — meaning the mechanism fails to pause the Agent when the threshold is reached due to a defect in Provider's platform code. Provider is not liable for:

    (a) Thresholds that Customer set too high for its risk tolerance.

    (b) Actions that individually fall below the threshold but collectively cause financial harm.

    (c) Third-party system delays that cause the monetary value of in-flight Actions to exceed the threshold before the pause takes effect.

triggers.main_clause:
SECTION 4: THE RAG-ONLY MANDATE

The architectural firewall that makes deletion possible. This is the centerpiece of this DPA.

4.1: Mandatory Processing Architecture

Provider warrants that Customer Personal Data shall be processed solely via Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG Processing). Specifically:

    (a) Vector Storage Only.
    Customer Personal Data that is processed for AI-powered features shall be stored as Embeddings in a Vector Store that is architecturally separate from the AI Model. The Vector Store allows for selective, granular deletion of individual data records.

    (b) No Fine-Tuning.
    Provider SHALL NOT use Customer Personal Data to Fine-Tune the weights of any AI Model — including but not limited to supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, LoRA, QLoRA, distillation, knowledge distillation, or any other technique that would cause Customer Personal Data to become encoded in Model Weights.

    (c) No Weight Modification.
    Provider SHALL NOT engage in any processing activity that causes Customer Personal Data, or patterns derived exclusively from Customer Personal Data, to be permanently encoded in the neural network parameters of any AI Model.

    (d) Frozen Model Guarantee.
    The AI Model used to process Customer Data shall remain "frozen" — meaning its weights are not modified by Customer-specific data during the provision of the Service to Customer. Model updates by the upstream AI Model Provider (general model improvements, safety patches, etc.) are permitted and do not violate this guarantee.

4.2: Why This Matters — The Disgorgement Risk

This section explains the commercial rationale in plain language.

Regulatory enforcement — including the FTC's orders in matters involving unauthorized use of consumer data for model training — has established that when personal data is used to fine-tune an AI model, the data becomes inseparable from the model's weights. Deletion of the source data does not remove the data's influence from the model. The only remedy available to regulators in such cases is "algorithmic disgorgement" — the forced deletion of the entire model.

The RAG-Only Mandate eliminates this risk entirely. Because Customer Data is stored externally in a Vector Store and never enters the model's weights, Customer Data can be selectively deleted without affecting the AI Model. This protects both Customer and Provider from disgorgement risk.

4.3: Verification

Upon Customer's written request (no more than once per calendar year, unless a specific incident warrants additional verification), Provider shall provide written confirmation that:

    i.   the RAG-Only Mandate has been maintained;

    ii.  no Customer Personal Data has been used for Fine-Tuning; and

    iii. the processing architecture described in Section 4.1 is in place.

Provider shall maintain technical documentation of its data processing architecture sufficient to demonstrate compliance with this Section 4. Such documentation shall be made available to Customer or Customer's qualified third-party auditor upon request, subject to Section 11 (Audit Rights).

4.4: Breach of the RAG-Only Mandate

Any breach of Section 4.1 — including any unauthorized Fine-Tuning of an AI Model using Customer Personal Data — constitutes:

    (a) a material breach of this DPA and the Main Terms;

    (b) grounds for immediate termination under Main Terms §12.2 (Termination for Cause), without cure period; and

    (c) a triggering event for Provider's indemnification obligations under Main Terms §10.1, to the extent the breach results in regulatory action, Data Subject claims, or third-party claims against Customer.

connected_mini_docs.excerpt:
§7.2 Vector Deletion Protocol

When Customer instructs Provider to erase a Data Subject's Personal Data, Provider shall delete source data, delete Embeddings from any Vector Store, purge caches, instruct relevant Sub-Processors, and provide written confirmation of erasure.

connected_mini_docs.title: DPA §10.2 Post-Termination Deletion
connected_mini_docs.excerpt:
§10.2 Post-Termination Deletion

Following the export window, Provider shall delete Personal Data from active systems, backup and archival systems, and all Embeddings generated from Customer Personal Data from all Vector Stores.

connected_mini_docs.title: DPA §12.2 DPA-Specific Liability
connected_mini_docs.excerpt:
§12.2 DPA-Specific Liability

Provider's liability for a breach of the RAG-Only Mandate, including unauthorized Fine-Tuning using Customer Personal Data, is not subject to the general liability cap.

connected_mini_docs.title: Vault intake
connected_mini_docs.excerpt:
Vault route

The intake must ask whether the system uses RAG, stateless pass-through, or fine-tuning; whether it uses a vector database; and which upstream model providers or sub-processors touch personal data.

annotations.label: Defensive control

Main clause:

```text
SECTION 4: THE RAG-ONLY MANDATE

The architectural firewall that makes deletion possible. This is the centerpiece of this DPA.

4.1: Mandatory Processing Architecture

Provider warrants that Customer Personal Data shall be processed solely via Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG Processing). Specifically:

    (a) Vector Storage Only.
    Customer Personal Data that is processed for AI-powered features shall be stored as Embeddings in a Vector Store that is architecturally separate from the AI Model. The Vector Store allows for selective, granular deletion of individual data records.

    (b) No Fine-Tuning.
    Provider SHALL NOT use Customer Personal Data to Fine-Tune the weights of any AI Model — including but not limited to supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, LoRA, QLoRA, distillation, knowledge distillation, or any other technique that would cause Customer Personal Data to become encoded in Model Weights.

    (c) No Weight Modification.
    Provider SHALL NOT engage in any processing activity that causes Customer Personal Data, or patterns derived exclusively from Customer Personal Data, to be permanently encoded in the neural network parameters of any AI Model.

    (d) Frozen Model Guarantee.
    The AI Model used to process Customer Data shall remain "frozen" — meaning its weights are not modified by Customer-specific data during the provision of the Service to Customer. Model updates by the upstream AI Model Provider (general model improvements, safety patches, etc.) are permitted and do not violate this guarantee.

4.2: Why This Matters — The Disgorgement Risk

This section explains the commercial rationale in plain language.

Regulatory enforcement — including the FTC's orders in matters involving unauthorized use of consumer data for model training — has established that when personal data is used to fine-tune an AI model, the data becomes inseparable from the model's weights. Deletion of the source data does not remove the data's influence from the model. The only remedy available to regulators in such cases is "algorithmic disgorgement" — the forced deletion of the entire model.

The RAG-Only Mandate eliminates this risk entirely. Because Customer Data is stored externally in a Vector Store and never enters the model's weights, Customer Data can be selectively deleted without affecting the AI Model. This protects both Customer and Provider from disgorgement risk.

4.3: Verification

Upon Customer's written request (no more than once per calendar year, unless a specific incident warrants additional verification), Provider shall provide written confirmation that:

    i.   the RAG-Only Mandate has been maintained;

    ii.  no Customer Personal Data has been used for Fine-Tuning; and

    iii. the processing architecture described in Section 4.1 is in place.

Provider shall maintain technical documentation of its data processing architecture sufficient to demonstrate compliance with this Section 4. Such documentation shall be made available to Customer or Customer's qualified third-party auditor upon request, subject to Section 11 (Audit Rights).

4.4: Breach of the RAG-Only Mandate

Any breach of Section 4.1 — including any unauthorized Fine-Tuning of an AI Model using Customer Personal Data — constitutes:

    (a) a material breach of this DPA and the Main Terms;

    (b) grounds for immediate termination under Main Terms §12.2 (Termination for Cause), without cure period; and

    (c) a triggering event for Provider's indemnification obligations under Main Terms §10.1, to the extent the breach results in regulatory action, Data Subject claims, or third-party claims against Customer.
```

```
- DPA §12.2 DPA-Specific Liability:

```

- Instrument route: DPA is the main instrument. Privacy Policy translates it for users. ToS routes liability/termination. DPIA and Vault intake operationalize it in review workflows.
- Reviewer note: This is the cleanest example of AI physics becoming legal architecture. The clause only works if the product architecture actually matches RAG-only processing.

triggers.main_clause:
6.2: Output — Conditional License

(a) The Copyright Reality.

Under current law, AI-generated content may not be eligible for copyright protection. In Thaler v. Vidal (Fed. Cir. 2024), the U.S. Court of Appeals affirmed that only natural persons can be "authors" under the Copyright Act. In Thaler v. Perlmutter (D.D.C. 2023), the court held that works generated entirely by AI without human authorship cannot be registered for copyright. The U.S. Copyright Office has confirmed that "the Office will not register works that are determined to have been generated by AI" absent sufficient human creative contribution.

This means: if you use Output as-is, without meaningful human modification, you may have no copyright in that Output. The more human creativity you contribute — through selection, arrangement, editing, and creative judgment — the stronger your claim to copyright in the resulting work. HITL is not only a safety obligation — it is your path to potential copyright ownership.

(b) License Grant.

Subject to your compliance with this Agreement — including the HITL Mandate (Section 5.1), the Acceptable Use Policy (Section 4), and payment of all Fees (Section 15) — Provider grants you a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, reproduce, modify, distribute, publicly display, and create derivative works from the Output for your business purposes.

(c) What This License Covers.

The license in (b) grants you the right to USE the Output. It does not — and cannot — grant you copyright ownership of the Output, because:

    i.   Provider does not hold copyright in AI-generated Output (the AI Model generated it, not Provider);

    ii.  the AI Model's provider may claim certain rights to Output under its own terms of service; and

    iii. as noted in (a), pure AI-generated content may not be copyrightable at all under current law.

What you CAN own is the copyright in derivative works you create by applying your own creative judgment to the Output — the edits you make, the selections you choose, the arrangement you design, the human creativity you add. The more transformative your contribution, the stronger your copyright position.

(d) Conditions of the License.

The Output license in (b) is conditioned on:

    i.   Compliance with this Agreement — any material breach of this Agreement may result in suspension or revocation of the Output license;

    ii.  HITL compliance — Output that has not been reviewed through the HITL process in Section 5.1 is used at your own risk and without the protections of the license;

    iii. Preservation of AI attribution — if the Output contains AI-generated content markers, watermarks, or metadata, you must preserve them in accordance with Section 4.1(b). Removal of such markers automatically revokes the license for the affected Output;

    iv.  Payment of Fees — the license is contingent on current payment status. If Fees become overdue beyond the grace period in Schedule B, Provider may suspend the Output license until payment is received; and

    v.   Compliance with third-party model terms — your use of Output must comply with the terms of service of the AI Model's provider as disclosed in Section 2.3(b). Provider is not responsible for restrictions imposed by the AI Model's provider on Output use.

(e) No Exclusivity.

The Output license is non-exclusive. The AI Model may generate similar or identical Output for other users in response to similar Input. Provider does not warrant that Output is unique to you. You have no claim against Provider or any other user if substantially similar Output is generated independently.

(f) Survival.

The Output license survives termination or expiration of this Agreement for Output generated during the term, provided you were in compliance with this Agreement at the time the Output was generated and you continue to comply with the conditions in (d)(iii) and (d)(v) after termination.

SECTION 2: SERVICE DESCRIPTION & AI DISCLOSURE

This section explains what the Service is, what powers it, how it may change, and what it is not. Read this section carefully — it sets the foundation for the warranties and disclaimers in Sections 8 and 9.

2.1: Nature of the Service

The Service is an assistive information tool that uses generative artificial intelligence to process your Input and produce Output.

The Service operates on a probabilistic basis — meaning the AI Model generates Output by predicting statistically likely sequences of text, code, or other content. It does not retrieve verified facts from a database, and it does not "understand" your Input in the way a human professional would. Different prompts — or even the same prompt at different times — may produce different Output.

The Service is designed to assist your decision-making process. It is not designed to replace it. You are responsible for evaluating, verifying, and approving all Output before relying on it (see Section 5.1 — HITL Mandate).

2.2: Service Classification

The Service is an information service. It is not a product, good, manufactured item, or deliverable.

No Output constitutes:

    (a) a finished work product ready for use without human review;

    (b) professional advice of any kind — including legal, medical, financial, tax, accounting, engineering, or therapeutic advice; or

    (c) a replacement for the judgment of a qualified human professional.

SECTION 6: CROSS-BORDER DATA TRANSFERS

How Personal Data moves across borders and the legal mechanisms that protect it.

6.1: Transfer Acknowledgment

Customer acknowledges that the provision of the Service may involve the transfer of Personal Data from Customer's jurisdiction to other jurisdictions, including:

    (a) Provider's jurisdiction: [INJECTION — Tally: "Where is your company based?"];

    (b) AI Model Provider's processing jurisdiction(s): [INJECTION — populated from Main Terms Schedule A]; and

    (c) other Sub-Processor jurisdictions as listed in Schedule C.

6.2: Transfer Mechanisms — EEA, UK, and Switzerland

Where Personal Data originating from the European Economic Area ("EEA"), United Kingdom, or Switzerland is transferred to a jurisdiction that has not received an adequacy determination from the relevant authority, Provider shall ensure that at least one of the following transfer mechanisms is in place:

    (a) Standard Contractual Clauses (EU).
    The Parties agree to the Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) approved by Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914. The applicable SCC Module is:

        i.   Module 2 (Controller to Processor): Applies to transfers of Customer Personal Data from Customer (Controller) to Provider (Processor). The completed SCCs are attached as Schedule D.

        ii.  Module 3 (Processor to Sub-Processor): Applies to onward transfers from Provider (Processor) to Sub-Processors, including the AI Model Provider. Provider shall ensure Module 3 SCCs (or equivalent protections) are in place with each Sub-Processor that processes EEA Personal Data in a non-adequate jurisdiction.

    (b) UK International Data Transfer Agreement (IDTA).
    For transfers of Personal Data originating from the United Kingdom, the UK IDTA (or the UK Addendum to the EU SCCs) shall apply, as issued by the UK Information Commissioner's Office under Section 119A of the UK Data Protection Act 2018.

    (c) Swiss FADP.
    For transfers of Personal Data originating from Switzerland, the EU SCCs shall apply with the modifications required by the Swiss Federal Data Protection and Information Commissioner, including references to Swiss law and supervisory authorities.

    (d) Adequacy Decisions.
    Transfers to jurisdictions that have received a valid adequacy determination from the European Commission, UK Secretary of State, or Swiss Federal Council (as applicable) do not require additional transfer mechanisms.

    (e) Other Approved Mechanisms.
    Binding Corporate Rules, codes of conduct, certification mechanisms, or other transfer mechanisms approved under Applicable Data Protection Law may be used where appropriate.

6.3: Transfer Impact Assessment

Where required by EDPB guidance or Applicable Data Protection Law, Customer may request that Provider cooperate in conducting a Transfer Impact Assessment to evaluate whether the legal framework of the destination country provides adequate protection for Personal Data. Provider shall provide reasonable cooperation, including information about:

    (a) access requests received from government authorities, to the extent permitted by law;

    (b) encryption and security measures in place; and

    (c) the legal regime applicable to Provider in the destination country.

6.4: US-Specific Transfer Provisions

EU-US Data Privacy Framework. Where Provider or Sub-Processors are certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF), such certification may serve as a valid transfer mechanism for transfers from the EEA to the United States, subject to ongoing validity of the European Commission's adequacy decision for the DPF.

Provider Disclosure. Provider shall disclose in Schedule C whether Provider or any Sub-Processor is certified under the EU-US DPF, UK Extension to the DPF, or Swiss-US DPF.

```text
SECTION 10: INDEMNIFICATION

10.1: Customer Indemnifies Provider

You agree to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Provider and its officers, directors, employees, agents, and affiliates ("Provider Indemnitees") from and against any and all claims, demands, losses, damages, liabilities, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to:

    (a) your breach of this Agreement, including the Acceptable Use Policy;

    (b) your violation of Section 5.4 (Prohibited Reliance Scenarios) or your failure to implement HITL as required by Section 5.1;

    (c) your Input infringing, misappropriating, or violating any third party's Intellectual Property Rights, privacy rights, or other rights;

    (d) your failure to disclose AI use to your end users as required by Section 3.4;

    (e) Minor access through your product in violation of Section 3.2;

    (f) your use of Output in a manner that causes harm to any third party — including but not limited to reliance claims by your end users, defamation claims, or IP infringement claims arising from your publication or distribution of Output;

    (g) any Autonomous Agent actions taken within your authorized parameters under Section 2.7, where the resulting harm arises from your configuration, deployment, or failure to monitor the Autonomous Agent; and

    (h) your violation of Applicable Law in connection with your use of the Service.

10.2: Provider Indemnifies Customer (Limited)

Provider agrees to indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Customer from and against any third-party claims that the Service itself — not the Output, not the AI Model, and not any third-party component — infringes a valid intellectual property right in the jurisdiction where Customer uses the Service.

Carve-outs. Provider's indemnification obligation under this Section 10.2 shall NOT apply to claims arising from:

    (a) Output — claims that AI-generated Output infringes third-party rights. This is disclaimed in Section 8.6 and is your responsibility under HITL;

    (b) The AI Model — claims arising from the AI Model itself, including its training data, algorithms, or behavior. This is the AI Model provider's responsibility, not Provider's;

    (c) Customer modifications — your modification, configuration, or customization of the Service beyond its intended use;

    (d) Combination — use of the Service in combination with third-party products, services, or data not provided by Provider, where the infringement arises from the combination;

    (e) Violation of this Agreement — use of the Service in violation of this Agreement, the AUP, or Provider's documentation; or

    (f) Continued use after notice — your continued use of an allegedly infringing aspect of the Service after Provider has provided a non-infringing alternative or has notified you to cease use.

Remedies. If the Service becomes the subject of an infringement claim, Provider may, at its sole option and expense:

    (i) obtain the right for you to continue using the Service;

    (ii) modify the Service to make it non-infringing; or

    (iii) if neither (i) nor (ii) is commercially practicable, terminate the affected portion of the Service and refund any prepaid Fees for the unused portion of the term.

10.3: Indemnification Procedure

Prompt Notice. The indemnified Party must provide prompt written notice of any claim to the indemnifying Party. Failure to provide prompt notice shall not relieve the indemnifying Party of its obligations except to the extent the indemnifying Party is materially prejudiced by the delay.

Control of Defense. The indemnifying Party shall have sole control over the defense and settlement of any indemnified claim — provided that:

    (i) the indemnifying Party shall not settle any claim in a manner that imposes liability on the indemnified Party or admits fault on behalf of the indemnified Party without the indemnified Party's prior written consent, not to be unreasonably withheld; and

    (ii) the indemnified Party may participate in the defense at its own expense with counsel of its choosing.

Cooperation. The indemnified Party shall cooperate fully with the indemnifying Party in the defense of any claim, including providing access to relevant records, personnel, and information.
```

```text
SECTION 9: LIMITATION OF LIABILITY

9.1: Liability Cap

TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, PROVIDER'S TOTAL AGGREGATE LIABILITY UNDER OR IN CONNECTION WITH THIS AGREEMENT — WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), STRICT LIABILITY, OR ANY OTHER LEGAL OR EQUITABLE THEORY — SHALL NOT EXCEED THE GREATER OF:

    (a) THE TOTAL FEES ACTUALLY PAID BY CUSTOMER TO PROVIDER IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY; OR

    (b) FIVE THOUSAND U.S. DOLLARS ($5,000 USD).

9.2: Exclusion of Consequential Damages

TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT PERMITTED BY APPLICABLE LAW, NEITHER PARTY SHALL BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY:

    (a) INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES;

    (b) LOSS OF PROFITS, REVENUE, BUSINESS, SAVINGS, OR GOODWILL;

    (c) LOSS OF DATA OR DATA BREACH COSTS, EXCEPT AS EXPRESSLY PROVIDED IN SECTION 7.6;

    (d) COST OF PROCUREMENT OF SUBSTITUTE SERVICES;

    (e) BUSINESS INTERRUPTION; OR

    (f) ANY OTHER DAMAGES NOT DIRECTLY CAUSED BY PROVIDER'S BREACH OF THIS AGREEMENT —

IN EACH CASE, REGARDLESS OF THE THEORY OF LIABILITY AND EVEN IF THE PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.

9.3: Exceptions to the Cap

The liability cap in Section 9.1 and the exclusion in Section 9.2 shall NOT apply to:

    (a) Customer's indemnification obligations under Section 10.1;

    (b) Customer's breach of Section 4.1 (Prohibited Conduct);

    (c) Customer's breach of Section 5.4 (Prohibited Reliance Scenarios);

    (d) Either Party's breach of Section 7.1 (Confidentiality) — up to a maximum of two times (2x) the liability cap in Section 9.1(a) or (b), whichever is greater;

    (e) Either Party's fraud, gross negligence, or willful misconduct; and

    (f) Any liability that cannot be limited by Applicable Law.

9.4: Essential Purpose

THE LIMITATIONS IN THIS SECTION 9 SHALL APPLY EVEN IF ANY LIMITED REMEDY PROVIDED IN THIS AGREEMENT FAILS OF ITS ESSENTIAL PURPOSE.

9.5: Jurisdictional Liability Floor

To the extent that Applicable Law in your jurisdiction prohibits the limitation of certain liabilities, such liabilities are hereby limited to the minimum scope required by such Applicable Law. No limitation in this Section 9 shall be construed to exclude or limit any liability that cannot be lawfully excluded or limited.
```

SERVICE CREDITS

What you get if we miss our commitments — and how to claim it.

5.1: Availability Credits

If Service Availability falls below the Availability Target in a Measurement Period, Customer is entitled to Service Credits as follows:

    [Credit schedule appears in Schedule A / Service Level Parameters.]

5.2: TTFT Credits

If the TTFT Target is not met in a Measurement Period, Customer is entitled to a Service Credit of 10% of monthly fees for that period.

5.3: Credit Cap

Total Service Credits in any single Measurement Period shall not exceed 30% of the monthly fees paid or payable by Customer for that period, regardless of the number or type of SLA failures.

5.4: No Double-Counting

If a single incident causes both an Availability failure and a TTFT failure, the resulting credits are cumulative but remain subject to the Credit Cap in Section 5.3.

5.5: Claim Process

To receive Service Credits, Customer must submit a written request to Provider within 30 days after the end of the Measurement Period in which the SLA failure occurred. The request must identify the dates and times of claimed Downtime or TTFT failures. Provider shall respond within 15 business days. If Provider confirms the SLA failure, credits will be applied to the next invoice. If no subsequent invoice is issued within 12 months of the credit being confirmed, the credit expires.

5.6: Sole and Exclusive Remedy

Service Credits are Customer's sole and exclusive remedy — and Provider's entire liability — for any failure to meet the Availability Target or the TTFT Target. Service Credits do not constitute damages and shall not be combined with any other claim for damages arising from the same downtime or performance event. This limitation is consistent with, and subject to, the liability framework in Main Terms §9.

triggers.main_clause:
SECTION 5: SUB-PROCESSORS

Who else touches Customer's Personal Data, and how Provider manages the chain.

5.1: General Authorization

Customer provides Provider with general written authorization to engage Sub-Processors to process Personal Data on Customer's behalf, subject to the requirements of this Section 5.

5.2: Sub-Processor List

Provider shall maintain a current list of Sub-Processors, including:

    (a) the Sub-Processor's name;

    (b) the nature of the processing performed; and

    (c) the Sub-Processor's processing location.

The current Sub-Processor list is set out in Schedule C and is also maintained at [INJECTION: URL to live sub-processor list, if applicable].

Provider shall update the Sub-Processor list before engaging any new Sub-Processor or making any material change to an existing Sub-Processor's role.

5.3: Notification and Objection

Provider shall notify Customer of any intended addition or replacement of a Sub-Processor at least fourteen (14) days before the new Sub-Processor begins processing Personal Data.

Customer may object to a new Sub-Processor on reasonable grounds related to data protection within fourteen (14) days of receiving notice. "Reasonable grounds" means demonstrable concerns about the Sub-Processor's data protection practices, security posture, or legal jurisdiction — not commercial preference.

If Customer objects, the Parties shall work in good faith to resolve the objection. Resolution may include Provider demonstrating adequate safeguards, implementing additional contractual protections, or proposing an alternative Sub-Processor.

If the Parties cannot resolve the objection within thirty (30) days, Customer may terminate the affected Service component in accordance with Main Terms §12.3 (Termination for Convenience) without penalty.

5.4: AI Model Provider as Sub-Processor

Customer acknowledges that the AI Model Provider identified in Main Terms Schedule A is a Sub-Processor. Customer Data, including Input containing Personal Data, is transmitted to the AI Model Provider's API for processing in order to generate Output. This transmission is a necessary part of the Service.

Provider shall:

    (a) disclose the identity and processing jurisdiction of the AI Model Provider in Schedule C;

    (b) select AI Model Providers that maintain data protection practices consistent with industry standards;

    (c) where available, configure API settings to minimize data retention by the AI Model Provider, including zero-data-retention modes, opt-out of training, and ephemeral processing; and

    (d) contractually require AI Model Providers to not use Customer Data to train their models, where such contractual terms are available.

Limitation. Provider does not control the AI Model Provider's internal data processing practices, data retention schedules, or infrastructure. Provider cannot guarantee the AI Model Provider's compliance with Applicable Data Protection Law. Customer acknowledges this limitation.

Model swaps — where Provider replaces the AI Model Provider or the specific AI Model used — trigger the notification obligation under Section 5.3, as these constitute a change in Sub-Processor, and the notification obligation under Main Terms §2.4(b).

5.5: Sub-Processor Contractual Requirements

Provider shall enter into a written agreement with each Sub-Processor that imposes data protection obligations no less protective than those set out in this DPA, including:

    (a) processing Personal Data only on documented instructions;

    (b) implementing appropriate technical and organizational security measures;

    (c) assisting with Data Subject rights requests to the extent applicable;

    (d) notifying Provider without undue delay of any Personal Data Breach;

    (e) deleting or returning Personal Data upon termination of the sub-processing relationship; and

    (f) allowing for audits, either directly or through Provider.

5.6: Provider Liability for Sub-Processors

Provider shall remain fully liable to Customer for the performance of each Sub-Processor's obligations under this DPA. Where a Sub-Processor fails to fulfill its data protection obligations, Provider shall be liable to Customer as if Provider had performed the processing itself — subject to the liability limitations in the Main Terms §9.

5.7: Emergency Sub-Processor Engagement

Notwithstanding the notification period in Section 5.3, Provider may engage a replacement Sub-Processor on an emergency basis — without prior notice — where the engagement is reasonably necessary to:

    (a) respond to an active or imminent security threat;

    (b) replace a Sub-Processor that has suffered a critical outage affecting Service availability;

    (c) comply with a legal or regulatory requirement that cannot be deferred; or

    (d) replace a Sub-Processor whose data protection practices have been found to be materially deficient, posing an immediate risk to Customer Personal Data.

In the event of an emergency engagement, Provider shall:

    (a) notify Customer as soon as reasonably practicable, and in any event within seventy-two (72) hours of the engagement;

    (b) provide the same information required for standard Sub-Processor notifications, including name, processing activity, and location; and

    (c) ensure the emergency Sub-Processor is bound by data protection obligations no less protective than those in this DPA.

Customer retains the right to object to an emergency Sub-Processor under Section 5.3(b)–(d) after receiving notice. If the objection cannot be resolved, Customer may terminate the affected Service component under Main Terms §12.3.

- Privacy Policy:
```text
Privacy route

The Privacy Policy explains AI processing, international transfers, and service-provider/subprocessor processing in user-facing language.
```
- Vault intake:
```text
Vault route

The intake must capture upstream model provider, processing jurisdictions, zero-retention availability, subprocessor list, and whether personal data is transmitted to model APIs.
```

Viewer annotations:
- Commercial problem: AI vendors often treat model providers as background infrastructure. For privacy contracting, the model provider may be a subprocessor where personal data is transmitted for AI processing.
- Authority / route basis: GDPR Art. 28 requires subprocessor authorization, notice, flow-down obligations, and liability. CPRA service-provider logic and DPDP/IT Act disclosure risk support the wider route.
- Defensive control: The DPA identifies the subprocessor chain, requires notice/objection, treats the AI model provider as a subprocessor, requires protective contracts, and handles emergency swaps.
- Instrument route: DPA is the main instrument. Privacy Policy translates the route for users. ToS incorporates the commercial gateway. Vault intake gathers the facts needed to populate schedules.
- Reviewer note: This is privacy contracting adapted to AI infrastructure. The question is not only “do we have a DPA?” It is whether the model-provider route is named, controlled, and operationally updateable.

triggers.main_clause:
ACCEPTABLE USE POLICY

How This Policy Is Organized

This AUP organizes use restrictions into three tiers:

Tier 1 — Prohibited Uses. Absolute prohibitions. No exceptions, no conditions, no waivers. Violation is a material breach of the ToS.

Tier 2 — Conditional Uses. Sensitive use cases that are permitted only if you meet the specific conditions listed. Failing to meet the conditions converts the use into a Tier 1 violation.

Tier 3 — Service Integrity. Rules that protect the Service's infrastructure, commercial model, and availability to all customers.

Consequences of Violation

Any violation of this AUP constitutes a material breach of the ToS. The consequence framework in ToS Section 4.3 applies to all AUP violations — including warranty voidance, suspension, termination for cause, content removal, and law enforcement reporting.

PROHIBITED USES (TIER 1)

These uses are absolutely prohibited. There are no exceptions, waivers, or conditions that make them permissible. Each prohibition supplements the Prohibited Conduct in ToS Section 4.1.

Illegal Content — Expanded Categories

In addition to the prohibitions in ToS Section 4.1(d), you must not use the Service to generate, distribute, or facilitate:

    (a) content that constitutes or facilitates fraud — including phishing communications, impersonation of real persons or institutions, fabricated credentials, or deceptive financial instruments;

    (b) content that facilitates human trafficking, forced labor, or exploitation — including recruitment materials, logistics coordination, or communications designed to coerce or deceive victims;

    (c) content that promotes, instructs in, or glorifies self-harm or suicide — including methods, encouragement, or content presenting self-harm as a solution to distress; or

    (d) content that constitutes targeted harassment, stalking, or coordinated abuse — including doxing materials or content designed to intimidate, threaten, or silence specific individuals.

Synthetic Media — Expanded Applications

In addition to the prohibitions in ToS Section 4.1(c), you must not use the Service to:

    (a) generate synthetic media for political manipulation — including fabricated speeches, falsified official statements, or AI-generated content designed to misattribute actions or positions to political figures, candidates, or public officials;

    (b) create synthetic media for financial fraud — including fabricated earnings calls, falsified executive communications, or AI-generated voice or video designed to manipulate markets or authorize fraudulent transactions; or

    (c) produce non-consensual intimate imagery — including any sexually explicit or intimate visual content depicting a real person without that person's verified, written consent, regardless of whether the content is generated from scratch or modified from existing imagery.

Election Interference

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) suppress voter turnout through fabricated announcements about changed polling locations, dates, or eligibility requirements;

    (b) impersonate election officials, government agencies, or candidates for the purpose of misleading voters;

    (c) produce or distribute fabricated polling data, election results, or ballot information; or

    (d) micro-target voters with personalized disinformation based on psychological profiling or inferred political vulnerabilities.

Autonomous Weapons & Lethal Decision Support

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) develop, train, or operate autonomous weapons systems — meaning systems that select and engage targets without meaningful human control over each individual engagement decision; or

    (b) provide kill-chain decision support — including target identification, threat assessment, or engagement recommendations in lethal-force contexts — without qualified human oversight at every decision point.

Mass Surveillance & Social Scoring

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) build or operate social scoring systems that evaluate individuals based on social behavior, personal characteristics, or inferred traits to determine access to services, benefits, or opportunities in a disproportionate or discriminatory manner;

    (b) conduct untargeted mass surveillance — meaning systematic, indiscriminate monitoring of populations using AI-powered analysis without a specific, lawful basis for each instance; or

    (c) deploy real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement purposes — except where explicitly authorized by Applicable Law and subject to prior judicial or independent administrative authorization.

Exploitation of Minors

In addition to ToS Section 4.1(d)(i), you must not use the Service to:

    (a) generate any sexual, romantic, or intimate content involving minors — real or fictional, regardless of stated purpose; or

    (b) create content designed to groom, recruit, or manipulate minors — including content that builds inappropriate emotional dependency, normalizes harmful behaviors, or isolates minors from trusted adults.

Evidence Fabrication

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) generate content intended for submission to courts, tribunals, arbitrators, or regulatory bodies as factual evidence or legal authority — without qualified human verification of every claim, citation, and factual assertion in the Output;

    (b) create fabricated case citations, statutory references, or regulatory guidance — whether for legal proceedings or publication where a reasonable reader would rely on the cited authority as genuine; or

    (c) produce AI-generated expert reports, declarations, or affidavits presenting AI-generated analysis as the independent professional judgment of a named human expert — without that expert's substantive review and approval.

AI Capability Misrepresentation

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) misrepresent AI capabilities to investors, customers, or regulators — including claiming your product is "fully autonomous" or "AI-powered" when the Service performs only limited or supplementary functions;

    (b) conceal human intervention — including marketing a product as automated AI when it relies substantially on human workers to perform tasks represented as AI-driven; or

    (c) attribute capabilities to the Service that the Service does not possess — including unsupported claims about accuracy rates, processing speeds, or analytical capabilities.

Algorithmic Collusion

You must not use the Service to:

    (a) coordinate pricing with competitors through AI pricing algorithms that access, share, or respond to competitor pricing data in a manner constituting price-fixing or bid-rigging under antitrust law; or

    (b) develop or deploy common pricing algorithms that align, coordinate, or coerce pricing decisions across competing businesses — whether through direct data sharing, signaling, or convergent algorithmic behavior.

triggers.main_clause:
SECTION 11: SUSPENSION

11.1: Right to Suspend

Provider may suspend your access to the Service, in whole or in part, if:

    (a) you are in material breach of this Agreement and have not cured such breach within any applicable cure period;

    (b) you have violated Section 4.1 (Prohibited Conduct) or the AUP;

    (c) your use of the Service poses a security threat to the Service, Provider's infrastructure, or other customers;

    (d) Fees are overdue beyond the grace period specified in Schedule B;

    (e) a government or regulatory authority orders or requests the suspension; or

    (f) your use of the Service threatens the integrity, performance, or availability of the Service for other customers.

11.2: Notice

With Notice. Where practicable, Provider shall provide written notice before suspending access, specifying the reason for suspension and the steps required to remedy the cause.

Without Notice. Provider may suspend access immediately without prior notice for violations of Section 4.1(d) (Illegal Content), security incidents under Section 7.6(c), or legal/regulatory orders that require immediate compliance. In such cases, Provider shall notify you as soon as reasonably practicable after the suspension.

11.3: Effect of Suspension

Fees continue to accrue during any suspension caused by your breach, violation, or non-payment. You are not entitled to any credit, refund, or SLA remedy for periods of suspension caused by your conduct.

Provider is not liable for any loss, damage, or inconvenience arising from suspension — provided Provider acts in good faith and in accordance with this Section 11.

Your obligations under this Agreement continue during suspension, including payment obligations and confidentiality duties.

11.4: Reinstatement

Provider shall reinstate your access promptly once:

    (a) the cause of suspension has been resolved to Provider's reasonable satisfaction; and

    (b) you provide adequate assurance of future compliance, if requested by Provider.

Provider may require a written remediation plan before reinstatement for repeated or serious violations.

SECTION 12: TERM & TERMINATION

12.1: Term

Initial Term. This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for the initial term specified in Schedule B.

Renewal. Unless otherwise specified in Schedule B, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive periods equal in length to the Initial Term, unless either Party gives written notice of non-renewal at least thirty (30) days before the end of the then-current term.

12.2: Termination for Cause

With Cure Period. Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party commits a material breach and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days of receiving written notice specifying the breach.

Without Cure Period / Immediate Termination. Provider may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice, without a cure period, if Customer:

    (a) violates Section 4.1 (Prohibited Conduct);

    (b) violates Section 5.4 (Prohibited Reliance Scenarios);

    (c) fails to pay Fees for a period exceeding sixty (60) days after the due date;

    (d) fails to disclose AI use to end users as required by Section 3.4; or

    (e) becomes subject to insolvency proceedings, bankruptcy, receivership, liquidation, or assignment for the benefit of creditors.

Mutual Immediate Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately if the other Party becomes subject to insolvency proceedings described above.

12.3: Termination for Convenience

Either Party may terminate this Agreement for any reason — or for no reason — by providing thirty (30) days' written notice to the other Party.

If Customer terminates for convenience during a prepaid term, no refund of prepaid Fees shall be due for the remainder of the term — unless Schedule B expressly provides otherwise.

If Provider terminates for convenience, Provider shall refund Customer a pro-rata portion of any prepaid Fees for the unused remainder of the term.

12.4: Effect of Termination

Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement:

    (a) Customer's right to access and use the Service terminates immediately, or at the end of the notice period, as applicable;

    (b) the Output license granted in Section 6.2 survives in accordance with Section 6.2(f);

    (c) all accrued rights and obligations survive, including the right to payment for Fees incurred before termination; and

    (d) each Party shall return or destroy the other Party's Confidential Information in accordance with Section 7.1, except as required by law or permitted by the survival provisions below.

12.5: Post-Termination Obligations

Customer. Within thirty (30) days of termination, Customer shall:

    (a) cease all use of the Service;

    (b) return or destroy Provider's Confidential Information;

    (c) pay all outstanding Fees; and

    (d) certify in writing, upon request, that it has complied with the foregoing.

Provider. Provider shall:

    (a) provide the data export window in Section 7.4(a);

    (b) delete Customer Data and Input in accordance with Section 7.4(b); and

    (c) provide written confirmation of deletion upon Customer's request.

12.6: Surviving Provisions

The following provisions survive termination or expiration of this Agreement: definitions, consequences of violation, prohibited reliance, intellectual property, data/privacy/confidentiality, disclaimers, limitation of liability, indemnification, effect of termination and post-termination obligations, force majeure for events occurring during the Term, dispute resolution, and general provisions.

    (c) your use of the Service poses a security threat to the Service, Provider's infrastructure, or other customers;

    (d) Fees are overdue beyond the grace period specified in Schedule B;

    (e) a government or regulatory authority orders or requests the suspension; or

    (f) your use of the Service threatens the integrity, performance, or availability of the Service for other customers.

11.2: Notice

With Notice. Where practicable, Provider shall provide written notice before suspending access, specifying the reason for suspension and the steps required to remedy the cause.

Without Notice. Provider may suspend access immediately without prior notice for violations of Section 4.1(d) (Illegal Content), security incidents under Section 7.6(c), or legal/regulatory orders that require immediate compliance. In such cases, Provider shall notify you as soon as reasonably practicable after the suspension.

11.3: Effect of Suspension

Fees continue to accrue during any suspension caused by your breach, violation, or non-payment. You are not entitled to any credit, refund, or SLA remedy for periods of suspension caused by your conduct.

Provider is not liable for any loss, damage, or inconvenience arising from suspension — provided Provider acts in good faith and in accordance with this Section 11.

Your obligations under this Agreement continue during suspension, including payment obligations and confidentiality duties.

11.4: Reinstatement

Provider shall reinstate your access promptly once:

    (a) the cause of suspension has been resolved to Provider's reasonable satisfaction; and

    (b) you provide adequate assurance of future compliance, if requested by Provider.

Provider may require a written remediation plan before reinstatement for repeated or serious violations.

SECTION 12: TERM & TERMINATION

12.1: Term

Initial Term. This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for the initial term specified in Schedule B.

Renewal. Unless otherwise specified in Schedule B, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive periods equal in length to the Initial Term, unless either Party gives written notice of non-renewal at least thirty (30) days before the end of the then-current term.

12.2: Termination for Cause

With Cure Period. Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party commits a material breach and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days of receiving written notice specifying the breach.

Without Cure Period / Immediate Termination. Provider may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice, without a cure period, if Customer:

    (a) violates Section 4.1 (Prohibited Conduct);

    (b) violates Section 5.4 (Prohibited Reliance Scenarios);

    (c) fails to pay Fees for a period exceeding sixty (60) days after the due date;

    (d) fails to disclose AI use to end users as required by Section 3.4; or

    (e) becomes subject to insolvency proceedings, bankruptcy, receivership, liquidation, or assignment for the benefit of creditors.

Mutual Immediate Termination. Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately if the other Party becomes subject to insolvency proceedings described above.

12.3: Termination for Convenience

Either Party may terminate this Agreement for any reason — or for no reason — by providing thirty (30) days' written notice to the other Party.

If Customer terminates for convenience during a prepaid term, no refund of prepaid Fees shall be due for the remainder of the term — unless Schedule B expressly provides otherwise.

If Provider terminates for convenience, Provider shall refund Customer a pro-rata portion of any prepaid Fees for the unused remainder of the term.

12.4: Effect of Termination

Upon termination or expiration of this Agreement:

    (a) Customer's right to access and use the Service terminates immediately, or at the end of the notice period, as applicable;

    (b) the Output license granted in Section 6.2 survives in accordance with Section 6.2(f);

    (c) all accrued rights and obligations survive, including the right to payment for Fees incurred before termination; and

    (d) each Party shall return or destroy the other Party's Confidential Information in accordance with Section 7.1, except as required by law or permitted by the survival provisions below.

12.5: Post-Termination Obligations

Customer. Within thirty (30) days of termination, Customer shall:

    (a) cease all use of the Service;

    (b) return or destroy Provider's Confidential Information;

    (c) pay all outstanding Fees; and

    (d) certify in writing, upon request, that it has complied with the foregoing.

Provider. Provider shall:

    (a) provide the data export window in Section 7.4(a);

    (b) delete Customer Data and Input in accordance with Section 7.4(b); and

    (c) provide written confirmation of deletion upon Customer's request.

12.6: Surviving Provisions

The following provisions survive termination or expiration of this Agreement: definitions, consequences of violation, prohibited reliance, intellectual property, data/privacy/confidentiality, disclaimers, limitation of liability, indemnification, effect of termination and post-termination obligations, force majeure for events occurring during the Term, dispute resolution, and general provisions.

Viewer annotations:
- Commercial problem: Termination is not one switch. Different AI failures require different shutdown rights: full account suspension, targeted agent deactivation, DPA component termination, SLA exclusion, or immediate termination.
- Authority / route basis: This is commercial enforcement architecture supported by AUP breach, security risk, regulatory order, data breach, agentic failure, and non-payment routes.
- Defensive control: The stack separates suspension, immediate no-notice suspension, cure-period termination, no-cure termination, convenience termination, data export/deletion, and survival.
- Instrument route: ToS is the main enforcement instrument. AUP supplies prohibited-use breach. AGT supplies targeted agent controls. DPA supplies privacy-specific termination. SLA excludes customer-caused downtime from credits.
- Reviewer note: This proves the stack can enforce AI governance, not merely describe it. A policy without suspension, termination, deactivation, and data-return routes is not operational.
